title: Angel Names
wikitext:
[]: Angel Names             Quote Post by ChronoX » Sat Jan 08, 2005 12:10 pm Where did all the names of the Angels come from? Did Anno just make them up or did he go to the bible or what??

[]:             Quote Post by Phaze » Sat Jan 08, 2005 7:10 pm Most, if not all of them, are biblical. Now someone make a post twice as large and in-depth as me. (at least 42 words.)

[]:             Quote Post by ChronoX » Sat Jan 08, 2005 8:11 pm thats what i figured.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 5:22 am Phaze wrote: Most, if not all of them, are biblical.

[]:             Quote Post by ChronoX » Sun Jan 09, 2005 12:02 pm yes it is

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sun Jan 09, 2005 1:36 pm Anno didn't make the names up, I can tell you that for certain. I've found them in online sources completely irrelevent to Eva. None of the Angels in Eva are in the bible, and it is my understanding that they're names come from secondary, non-canonical sources. I don't know much about apocrypha and what not, so I can't really comment on that.

[]:             Quote Post by Xanatose » Sun Jan 09, 2005 2:16 pm A question right up my angle. Here we go... As has been stated, none of the angel names in NGE are fictional. All are based on apocrypha sources. The Apocrypha are collections of folk stories tied to the evolution of the Jewish, Christian, and Islamic religions at the hip. While religious officals would only cannonize certain stories based on whether or not it was politically expident (and here it becomes necessary to understand the politics of the world from the rise of Sumeria to the middle ages) many stories about God and assorted angels flurished. These three religions are not islands onto themselves. During their formative years they all borrowed from other mystical and religious ideas in neighborning countries. Because these extra-cannon sources were seen as a t different times as infiltration by pagans or embraced as tales to safe-guard the faithful in dangerious times, some made the cut and others were discarded. Examples of this are various angels named after Pagan gods, but recognized as demons or fallen angels by the Hebrew tribes (and here we can obviously see the inherent influince of politics in this situation, where your enemy's god is reduced to a mere impish demon in the face of your god.) And an example of a benificial borrow would be the transformation of the original incarnation of God as one of many in Sumeria, where Abraham was born into Polytheism, and the eventual acception of the Persion belief in the eternal batttle of light vs darkness which lead to Yahweh being The One against All Others that helped Jews cope with their forced emmigration to Babylon when Israel was conqoured. (Again, politics comes into play: they could only conqours us because we have failed in the eyes of our god, but our god is still powerful even in this land of foreign gods, because ours is The True God of all!) In the ancient eras it was common to live next to neighbors who believed in more than one god, and just as common to be sympahetic to them at the least, or directly influenced by them all together. An example is in Egypt. There we find a history of accepting more than one god, and when Christanity spread there, people were tought about the one god model, but still expected patrons to pray to. Angels became the model by which ancient customs of patron rituals could be allowed. Rites were created to contact angels, and pendents were fashioned baring their names to ward off evil creatures, such as Lillith. To expand on an example of a legend emerging within the religion that was later discarded, look to Lillith and the unnamed virgin -- the two original wives said to have lived before Eve. They were created to explain what the process was that led to god making the mother of humanity, but only because a certain fundamentalist shift took place within the religion for a period and people wanted more literal answers in their origin myth. Once this appetite was satisfied, people eventually stopped carring, and today no one is really taught about Lillith. The same goes for Satan, but we can still see the effects of this fictional angel's creation being played out (Satan is a relatively new angel, only created in the last two thousand years. Before that there was no offical enemy to god, and all angels were in his service, including angels of Evil.) I highly recommend that if you're interested in looking up information on angels and their origins, read " A Dictionary of Angels: Including The Fallen Ones " by Gustov Davidson

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 2:24 pm I'll tell you one thing: Christianity really gives angels the short end of the stick. Stick to Judaic stuff and your research will be considerably more fulfilling. (Remember, Jewish mysticism is the main influence on NGE, not Christianity as a lot of people like to think.) Many of our old friends are mentioned in The Legends of the Jews . (The two volumes of footnotes aren't online, unfortunately; I remember reading about Zeruel in one of them.) A Dictionary of Angels by Gustav Davidson is a nice book to have, as well, and at least mentions most of them (Ireul seemed to have slipped past him).

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 2:32 pm Xanatose wrote: To expand on an example of a legend emerging within the religion that was later discarded, look to Lillith and the unnamed virgin -- the two original wives said to have lived before Eve. They were created to explain what the process was that led to god making the mother of humanity, but only because a certain fundamentalist shift took place within the religion for a period and people wanted more literal answers in their origin myth.

[]:             Quote Post by Xanatose » Sun Jan 09, 2005 2:45 pm Reichu wrote: Xanatose wrote: To expand on an example of a legend emerging within the religion that was later discarded, look to Lillith and the unnamed virgin -- the two original wives said to have lived before Eve. They were created to explain what the process was that led to god making the mother of humanity, but only because a certain fundamentalist shift took place within the religion for a period and people wanted more literal answers in their origin myth.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 3:12 pm Xanatose wrote: Well, then what did you hear? I try not to make comments unless I'm sure of my answers, but I may be wrong -- I make no claim for perfection -- but I'd still like to be corrected if you could do that.

[]:             Quote Post by Xanatose » Sun Jan 09, 2005 3:32 pm Reichu wrote: I am certainly no experts, but my 'research' gave me the impression that Lilith was originally a monster (demoness, even) of the night, and only attained her popular role as Adam's first wife when the author of the Alphabet of Ben Sira (a highly disputed and non-rabbinical work, apparently) wrote her as such. <snip>

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 3:48 pm Xanatose wrote: From my research, the "ample" enough reason to identify Satan and Sammael together seems to be along the same lines as that which binds Lucifer and Satan

[]:             Quote Post by Xanatose » Sun Jan 09, 2005 3:59 pm Reichu wrote: Xanatose wrote: From my research, the "ample" enough reason to identify Satan and Sammael together seems to be along the same lines as that which binds Lucifer and Satan

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jan 09, 2005 7:24 pm Xanatose wrote: I believe I just made the same statements you did. That the equation of Lucifer and Satan is incorrect, but that throughout time they've been associated by position. Satan was chief adversary to God, and Lucifer falsely as the same -- and thus they became the same angel, dispite the error.

[]:             Quote Post by Phaze » Sun Jan 09, 2005 8:19 pm Reichu wrote: Try finding any of them (Adam aside) in the Bible. I dare you.

[]:             Quote Post by Xanatose » Sun Jan 09, 2005 9:52 pm Reichu wrote: But... Lucifer never WAS an angel. Did you read that thread I linked to?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Jan 10, 2005 8:23 am Xanatose wrote: You associate Lucifer with Nebuchadnezzar. That's fine. The association with Ishtar and Neb'by is indispute, but it works both ways, and continues along the thread I've been harping on.

[]:             Quote Post by Hexon.Arq » Mon Jan 10, 2005 3:12 pm Ishtar is connected to Lucifer? Splendid. She, or rather, Semiramis, had that wonderful connection with her son, Nimrod, no? The one after which many mother-son deifications were crafted after (unless I'm mistaken and you can enlighten me). Yay Oedipal complex! Go Yuicifer!

[]:             Quote Post by chocomanji » Mon Jan 10, 2005 10:56 pm Xanatose wrote: I highly recommend that if you're interested in looking up information on angels and their origins, read " A Dictionary of Angels: Including The Fallen Ones " by Gustov Davidson
